description: A visual overview of VLM capabilities, likely showing icons or examples of tasks such as visual question answering, image captioning, document understanding, and web page analysis
teaching_value: Gives students an immediate concrete sense of the breadth of tasks VLMs can perform, anchoring abstract definitions to real use cases before diving into architecture
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a VLM lesson to motivate why these models matter and what problems they solve before introducing technical details
suggested_caption: VLMs handle diverse tasks from captioning to document understanding. (HuggingFace Blog)

description: Screenshot of the Vision Arena leaderboard interface, showing a side-by-side anonymous model comparison UI where users submit an image and prompt and vote on preferred outputs
teaching_value: Illustrates how human-preference-based evaluation works in practice, contrasting it with metric-based leaderboards and teaching students about evaluation methodology choices
when_to_show: Show when discussing how to select or compare VLMs, especially to contrast human-preference evaluation against automated benchmark metrics
suggested_caption: Vision Arena ranks VLMs via anonymous human preference voting. (HuggingFace Blog)

description: Screenshot of the Open VLM Leaderboard showing a ranked table of vision language models with scores across multiple benchmark metrics, with filtering options for model size and license type
teaching_value: Shows students how to navigate a real evaluation resource, understand multi-metric ranking, and apply filters to find models suited to specific constraints like size or open-source requirements
when_to_show: Show when teaching model selection criteria, especially when students need to choose a VLM for a project with specific size or licensing constraints
suggested_caption: Open VLM Leaderboard ranks models across benchmarks with size and license filters. (HuggingFace Blog)

description: Architecture diagram of a typical vision language model showing the sequential pipeline: image encoder → multimodal embedding projector → text decoder, with annotations indicating which components are frozen vs trained during pretraining
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the three-component VLM architecture and the two-stage training strategy (projector-only first, then projector+decoder), making abstract architectural descriptions concrete and memorable
when_to_show: Show when introducing VLM architecture, especially before or alongside explaining LLaVA's training procedure to ground the text description in a visual schema
suggested_caption: VLM architecture: image encoder, projector, and text decoder stacked sequentially. (HuggingFace Blog)

description: Detailed diagram labeled 'Structure of a Typical Vision Language Model' showing how image features from the encoder are projected into the text embedding space via a multimodal projector and combined with text tokens before being fed to the language model decoder
teaching_value: Explains the critical alignment mechanism between vision and language modalities at the token/embedding level, helping students understand how heterogeneous inputs are unified for a single decoder
when_to_show: Show after introducing the high-level architecture diagram to zoom into how image and text representations are actually merged, especially when explaining the projector training stage
suggested_caption: Projector aligns image features with text embeddings for the language decoder. (HuggingFace Blog)

description: Architecture diagram of BLIP-2 showing the three-component pipeline: a frozen pre-trained image encoder on the left, a lightweight 12-layer Querying Transformer (Q-Former) in the middle acting as a bridge, and a frozen large language model (LLM) on the right, with arrows indicating the flow of visual information through the system.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies how BLIP-2 achieves parameter efficiency by keeping both the image encoder and LLM frozen while only training the lightweight Q-Former bridge, helping students understand why the model needs 54x fewer trainable parameters than Flamingo while still achieving superior zero-shot performance.
when_to_show: Show this diagram when introducing BLIP-2 to explain the modular three-stage architecture before discussing training objectives or benchmarks; also use it when contrasting BLIP-2's efficiency against larger models like Flamingo.
suggested_caption: BLIP-2 architecture: frozen image encoder, Q-Former bridge, and frozen LLM. (Li et al.)

description: A visual showing multimodal AI applications in healthcare, likely depicting how different data types (medical images, text reports, audio) are combined for clinical tasks such as diagnosis, radiology, or patient monitoring.
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract concept of multimodality in a high-stakes real-world domain, helping students understand why combining modalities is not just convenient but necessary for certain applications.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the motivation for multimodal AI — specifically when arguing that some use cases are impossible with a single modality.
suggested_caption: Multimodal AI in healthcare: combining images, text, and signals. (Huyen Chip)

alt: Some cool multimodal use cases from GPT-4V
description: A showcase of GPT-4V use cases demonstrating multimodal capabilities, likely showing examples where the model processes images alongside text to answer questions, describe scenes, or perform reasoning.
teaching_value: Provides concrete, motivating examples of what multimodal models can do, helping students connect theoretical concepts to tangible outputs before diving into architecture details.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on multimodal AI to build intuition and motivation before introducing technical content.
suggested_caption: GPT-4V use cases illustrating multimodal reasoning and interaction. (Huyen Chip)

description: An example of GPT-4V performing document processing or OCR, showing the model reading and interpreting text within an image, such as a scanned document or form.
teaching_value: Illustrates a specific multimodal task (OCR/document understanding) and connects it to the classification framing — showing how recognizing characters is a finite-class prediction problem.
when_to_show: Show when explaining classification as a multimodal task type, particularly to contrast fixed-class outputs with more flexible generative approaches.
suggested_caption: GPT-4V performing OCR and document processing on an image. (Huyen Chip)

description: A diagram illustrating CLIP's zero-shot image classification capability, showing how text prompts (e.g., 'a photo of a dog') are matched against image embeddings to classify images without task-specific training.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the power of a shared embedding space — students can see how CLIP enables zero-shot transfer by comparing text and image embeddings rather than using a fixed classifier head.
when_to_show: Show when introducing CLIP's capabilities and the concept of zero-shot classification, before explaining the architecture that makes it possible.
suggested_caption: CLIP enables zero-shot image classification via shared text-image embeddings. (Huyen Chip)

description: A high-level architecture diagram of OpenAI's CLIP model, showing the dual-encoder structure: an image encoder (ResNet or ViT) and a text encoder (Transformer), both projecting inputs into a shared embedding space.
teaching_value: Provides the structural blueprint of CLIP, allowing students to understand how two separate encoders are aligned into one space — foundational for understanding contrastive training and downstream use in models like Flamingo and LLaVA.
when_to_show: Show when explaining CLIP's architecture in detail, after motivating why a shared embedding space is useful.
suggested_caption: CLIP's dual-encoder architecture maps images and text to a shared space. (Huyen Chip)

description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting a classifier objective (predicting one of N fixed classes) versus a language model objective (generating free-form text), illustrating their respective output spaces and limitations.
teaching_value: Clarifies a key conceptual distinction: why classifier-based training limits zero-shot generalization, and why language model objectives are more flexible but harder to train — motivating the contrastive approach CLIP uses.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP moved away from both classifier and language model objectives toward contrastive learning.
suggested_caption: Classifier vs. language model objectives: fixed classes vs. flexible text generation. (Huyen Chip)

alt: Multiple captions for the same image
description: An illustration showing multiple different human-written captions for the same image (from Flickr30K), demonstrating that a single image can be validly described in many ways.
teaching_value: Provides the key intuition for why exact-text prediction is a poor training objective — students see concretely that many captions are equally valid, motivating the switch to contrastive learning.
when_to_show: Show immediately before introducing contrastive learning, to build the intuition for why predicting exact captions is problematic.
suggested_caption: Multiple valid captions for one image motivate contrastive over generative objectives. (Huyen Chip)

description: A diagram showing how CLIP's contrastive learning works: for a batch of N (image, text) pairs, an N×N similarity matrix is computed, and the model is trained to maximize similarity along the diagonal (correct pairs) and minimize it off-diagonal.
teaching_value: Visually explains the core contrastive training mechanism — the similarity matrix makes it easy to see the two classification tasks (image-to-text and text-to-image retrieval) embedded in each training batch.
when_to_show: Show as the central visual when explaining CLIP's contrastive objective — use it to walk through the N×N matrix and the dual classification framing.
suggested_caption: CLIP's contrastive training: maximize diagonal similarity in an N×N pair matrix. (Huyen Chip)

description: Pseudocode for CLIP's contrastive training loop, showing how image and text embeddings are computed, normalized, the similarity matrix is formed, and the symmetric cross-entropy loss is calculated.
teaching_value: Bridges the conceptual diagram to implementation — students who understand the diagram can now see exactly how it translates to code, reinforcing understanding of the loss function and the role of the temperature parameter β.
when_to_show: Show after the similarity matrix diagram (image 8) for students ready to go deeper — ideal for those with a programming background or studying model implementation.
suggested_caption: CLIP training pseudocode: symmetric contrastive loss over image-text embeddings. (Huyen Chip)

description: A chart or graph showing the training efficiency improvement of CLIP's contrastive objective compared to the language model objective baseline, illustrating the reported 12x efficiency gain.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for why contrastive learning was chosen — students can see the quantitative payoff of the design decision, reinforcing that architectural choices have measurable consequences.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the contrastive objective to reinforce its practical advantage with data — useful when students ask 'why does this matter?'
suggested_caption: Contrastive learning gives CLIP a 12x efficiency gain over language model objectives. (Huyen Chip)

alt: CLIP as a strong baseline for image classification
description: Bar chart or performance comparison showing CLIP's image classification accuracy as a strong zero-shot baseline, likely compared to supervised models across multiple datasets, illustrating the 12x efficiency improvement from contrastive vs. language model objectives.
teaching_value: Helps students understand why contrastive learning objectives outperform language model objectives for vision tasks, and quantifies CLIP's practical efficiency gains.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP uses contrastive loss instead of a language model objective, and when discussing zero-shot transfer performance benchmarks.
suggested_caption: CLIP achieves strong zero-shot classification via contrastive learning efficiency. (Radford et al.)

description: Architecture diagram of unCLIP (DALL-E 2), showing how CLIP's joint image-text embedding space is used in a generative pipeline: a text encoder produces embeddings, a prior maps text to image embeddings, and a diffusion decoder generates images.
teaching_value: Illustrates how CLIP embeddings can be repurposed for image generation, bridging understanding of discriminative and generative uses of shared embedding spaces.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from CLIP as a classification/retrieval tool to its role enabling generative models, especially when explaining DALL-E 2's architecture.
suggested_caption: unCLIP uses CLIP embeddings to condition image generation via a diffusion decoder. (Ramesh et al.)

description: Screenshot or illustration of multi-turn conversations with Flamingo, showing the model responding to image-based questions and prompts, demonstrating its few-shot visual question answering and dialogue capabilities.
teaching_value: Concretely demonstrates what a Large Multimodal Model can do — answering questions about images in natural language — making abstract architecture concepts tangible for students.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the Flamingo section to motivate why the architecture is needed, before diving into technical components.
suggested_caption: Flamingo engages in multi-turn visual conversations using few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al.)

description: High-level architecture diagram of Flamingo showing its two main components: a frozen vision encoder (CLIP-like) that produces visual features, and a large language model (Chinchilla) augmented with cross-attention layers to condition text generation on visual inputs.
teaching_value: Gives students a clear mental model of how Flamingo combines vision and language components, showing which parts are frozen vs. trained and how information flows between modalities.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing Flamingo's architecture, as the anchor diagram for all subsequent component-level explanations.
suggested_caption: Flamingo combines a frozen vision encoder with a language model via cross-attention. (Alayrac et al.)

description: Table or diagram summarizing Flamingo's 4 training datasets: ALIGN and LTIP (image-text pairs), VTP (video-text pairs), and an interleaved image-text dataset (LTIP/M3W), including dataset sizes and training weights.
teaching_value: Shows students how large-scale multimodal training requires diverse data sources and that dataset weighting is a critical design choice affecting model performance.
when_to_show: Show when discussing Flamingo's training data strategy, particularly to contrast dataset scale with CLIP and to explain the role of dataset weights in the loss function.
suggested_caption: Flamingo trains on four datasets totaling 2.1B+ image-text pairs with tuned weights. (Alayrac et al.)

description: Diagram illustrating how Flamingo's language model (Chinchilla) generates text tokens conditioned on both preceding text tokens and visual tokens, showing the modified autoregressive text generation process with visual conditioning.
teaching_value: Clarifies the key difference between a standard language model and Flamingo's LM: the addition of visual token conditioning in the next-token prediction process.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how Flamingo extends a standard language model to handle visual inputs, before introducing Perceiver Resampler and GATED XATTN-DENSE.
suggested_caption: Flamingo predicts next text tokens conditioned on both text and visual inputs. (Alayrac et al.)

description: Diagram of Flamingo's Perceiver Resampler module, showing how variable-length visual features from the vision encoder (images or video frames) are compressed into a fixed set of 64 visual output tokens via cross-attention with learned latent queries.
teaching_value: Explains a key architectural innovation solving the variable-length visual input problem, helping students understand how Perceiver Resampler enables consistent interface between vision encoder and language model.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how Flamingo handles variable numbers of image/video features, specifically to illustrate the role of Perceiver Resampler before discussing GATED XATTN-DENSE.
suggested_caption: Perceiver Resampler compresses variable visual features into 64 fixed visual tokens. (Alayrac et al.)

description: Diagram of Flamingo's GATED XATTN-DENSE layers inserted between frozen Chinchilla LM layers, showing how cross-attention allows LM layers to attend to visual tokens, with a gating mechanism controlling the contribution of visual information.
teaching_value: Illustrates a critical architectural technique for injecting visual information into a frozen language model without catastrophic forgetting, and quantifies its importance (4.2% score drop without it).
when_to_show: Show after Perceiver Resampler to complete the picture of how visual tokens are integrated into text generation, emphasizing the ablation result showing its necessity.
suggested_caption: GATED XATTN-DENSE layers inject visual context into frozen LM layers via gated cross-attention. (Alayrac et al.)

description: Comparison table or diagram contrasting CLIP and Flamingo across key dimensions: task type (classification/retrieval vs. text generation), architecture, training data, and capabilities, serving as a summary of both models.
teaching_value: Provides a structured side-by-side comparison helping students consolidate understanding of both models and see how Flamingo builds upon and extends CLIP's foundations.
when_to_show: Show as a consolidation exercise after covering both CLIP and Flamingo, or as a review tool before moving to newer LMM architectures.
suggested_caption: CLIP vs. Flamingo: contrasting architectures, objectives, and capabilities in vision-language AI.

description: Diagram of ImageBind showing a unified embedding space where six modalities (images, text, audio, depth, thermal, IMU) are all aligned together using image as the binding modality, enabling cross-modal retrieval and reasoning.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the frontier direction of extending multimodal systems beyond image-text pairs to a universal embedding space, helping students understand scalable multimodal alignment strategies.
when_to_show: Show when discussing future directions in multimodal AI, specifically to illustrate how the field is moving toward unified representations across more than two modalities.
suggested_caption: ImageBind aligns six modalities into one shared embedding space using images as anchor. (Girdhar et al.)

description: Architecture diagram of LaVIN, showing how a frozen pretrained language model and vision encoder are connected via lightweight adapter modules to enable instruction-following with minimal training from scratch
teaching_value: Illustrates the adapter-based approach to multimodal instruction-following, helping students see how existing pretrained models can be efficiently combined without full retraining
when_to_show: When introducing efficient multimodal training strategies, specifically to contrast with Flamingo's heavier train-from-scratch approach
suggested_caption: LaVIN uses lightweight adapters to connect frozen vision and language encoders.

description: Diagram comparing multiple adapter-based architectures for large multimodal models, showing how different works position adapter layers between frozen vision encoders and language models to reduce trainable parameters
teaching_value: Gives students a comparative visual overview of the adapter design space, making it easier to understand the trade-offs between different efficient multimodal training approaches
when_to_show: When teaching the landscape of efficient multimodal training methods, after introducing BLIP-2 as a motivating example of parameter efficiency gains
suggested_caption: Adapter architectures for LMMs reduce trainable parameters while preserving pretrained knowledge. (Source: Chunyuan Li, CVPR 2023)

description: Second diagram from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial showing additional adapter-based LMM architectures, likely detailing specific design choices such as where adapters are inserted and how modalities are aligned
teaching_value: Extends the comparative view of adapter strategies, helping students understand the variety of design decisions (placement, size, connectivity) that affect multimodal model efficiency
when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 2 as a continuation of the adapter landscape overview, before transitioning to multimodal output generation
suggested_caption: Further adapter designs for LMMs from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial.

description: Diagram illustrating CM3's approach to multimodal output generation, where the model produces HTML markup as an intermediate text representation that encodes both text content and image references, which are then compiled into full webpages
teaching_value: Concretely demonstrates the 'text as intermediate output' strategy for multimodal generation, showing students how structured markup can bridge language models and image content
when_to_show: When introducing the two strategies for multimodal output (text intermediate vs. multimodal tokens), use this to illustrate the text-based intermediate approach
suggested_caption: CM3 generates HTML markup as intermediate output, enabling text and image webpage synthesis.

description: Screenshot or example output showing GPT-4V generating LaTeX code from an input image of a data table, demonstrating how a vision-language model can produce structured text markup that reconstructs the original visual content
teaching_value: Provides a concrete real-world example of text-as-intermediate-output for multimodal generation, making the abstract concept tangible by showing actual LaTeX code produced from a visual input
when_to_show: Use alongside the CM3 diagram when explaining text-based intermediate outputs; this example grounds the concept with a familiar, practical tool (GPT-4V)
suggested_caption: GPT-4V converts a data table image into LaTeX code, a text-intermediate multimodal output.

description: Diagram from 'Generating Images with Multimodal Language Models' (Koh et al., 2023) showing how an LMM interleaves text token generation and image generation/retrieval in a single output stream using tagged multimodal tokens
teaching_value: Visually explains the multimodal token approach to mixed-modality output, helping students understand how a single model can produce coherent sequences of text and images by tagging tokens by type
when_to_show: After explaining the text-intermediate approach (CM3, GPT-4V), introduce this to contrast with the multimodal token strategy, as described by Caiming Xiong
suggested_caption: LMMs generate interleaved text and images using tagged multimodal tokens. (Koh et al., 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of VisualBERT showing how text token embeddings and visual region embeddings (each composed of tokenized features f_o, segmentation embedding f_s, and position embedding f_p) are concatenated and fed into a BERT-style transformer with self-attention layers.
teaching_value: Shows students concretely how visual features are mapped into the same embedding space as text tokens, enabling a single transformer to process multimodal input — illustrating the 'images as tokens' paradigm.
when_to_show: When introducing the 'jointly training with image and text' approach; use to explain how visual regions are embedded analogously to word tokens before entering the transformer.
suggested_caption: VisualBERT fuses text and image region embeddings into a shared BERT encoder. (Li et al. 2019)

description: Ablation study table for VisualBERT on the NLVR benchmark, showing performance metrics across different model configurations (e.g., with/without visual grounding objectives, with/without pre-training).
teaching_value: Demonstrates empirically which components of VisualBERT contribute most to performance, teaching students how to interpret ablation studies and understand the importance of each design choice.
when_to_show: After explaining VisualBERT's training objectives; use to discuss which pre-training strategies matter and how researchers validate architectural decisions.
suggested_caption: VisualBERT ablation on NLVR shows impact of each training component. (Li et al. 2019)

description: Architecture diagram of SimVLM showing image patches processed by a ResNet convolutional stage into prefix tokens fed into a bidirectional encoder, followed by a causal (autoregressive) text decoder that generates output text.
teaching_value: Illustrates the prefix language model design where visual tokens serve as the prefix with full bidirectional attention, while text generation uses causal attention — helping students understand the asymmetric attention design.
when_to_show: When contrasting SimVLM with VisualBERT; use to explain how the prefix LM paradigm differs from full bidirectional models and why it suits image-conditioned text generation.
suggested_caption: SimVLM uses image patches as prefix tokens with bidirectional encoder and causal decoder. (Wang et al. 2022)

description: Ablation study table for SimVLM on VQA, comparing performance across variants: different pre-training objectives (PrefixLM vs. span corruption vs. naive LM), with/without image-text data, and with/without text-only data.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students that combining image-text and text-only data improves performance, and that the PrefixLM objective outperforms alternatives — reinforcing the importance of training data mix and objective choice.
when_to_show: After explaining SimVLM's training setup; use to discuss why mixing data modalities and choosing the right pre-training objective are critical design decisions.
suggested_caption: SimVLM ablation on VQA: PrefixLM and mixed data yield best results. (Wang et al. 2022)

description: Diagram illustrating the CM3 causally-masked multimodal modeling approach, showing how HTML web pages with embedded images are tokenized (images via VQVAE-GAN into discrete tokens), masked, and processed by a causal language model to reconstruct masked spans.
teaching_value: Shows students how a unified sequence model can handle both text and image tokens from structured web data, and how masking objectives extend to multimodal content — illustrating a generalized sequence-to-sequence multimodal approach.
when_to_show: When introducing CM3 as an alternative multimodal approach; use to contrast discrete image tokenization (VQVAE-GAN) with patch-based methods and explain how web-scale HTML data enables multimodal learning.
suggested_caption: CM3 tokenizes images and HTML text into a unified causal masked sequence model. (Aghajanyan et al. 2022)

description: Two-panel diagram: (left) Frozen model training pipeline showing only the vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) being updated while the language model parameters remain frozen, with image embeddings prepended as prefix to text; (right) test-time pipeline showing few-shot prompting with image-text pairs.
teaching_value: Visually distinguishes which parameters are trained vs. frozen, making the prefix-tuning paradigm concrete and showing how a frozen LM can be adapted for vision tasks without catastrophic forgetting.
when_to_show: When introducing the 'learned image embedding as frozen LM prefix' paradigm; use to contrast with full fine-tuning and explain why freezing the LM preserves its language capabilities.
suggested_caption: Frozen trains only the vision encoder; the LM stays frozen during training and inference. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)

description: Bar charts comparing performance of Frozen model variants on VQAv2 (left) and OKVQA (right): Frozen (LM frozen), Frozen finetuned (LM fine-tuned), Frozen scratch (trained from scratch), and Frozen train-blind (image blacked out).
teaching_value: Counterintuitively shows that fine-tuning the LM hurts VQA performance and that training from scratch fails entirely, reinforcing why preserving pre-trained LM knowledge is critical and how even a blind baseline scores non-trivially.
when_to_show: After explaining the Frozen architecture; use to spark discussion about why fine-tuning a pre-trained LM can be harmful and what the train-blind baseline reveals about LM prior knowledge.
suggested_caption: Frozen outperforms fine-tuned LM on VQA, showing value of preserving pre-trained knowledge. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)

description: Pipeline diagram of ClipCap showing: CLIP vision encoder producing image embeddings → lightweight mapping network F (transformer or MLP) → k prefix embedding vectors → frozen GPT-2 language model generating captions.
teaching_value: Illustrates how a minimal trainable mapping network bridges two frozen pre-trained models (CLIP and GPT-2), teaching students about modality alignment and parameter-efficient transfer learning.
when_to_show: When comparing ClipCap to Frozen; use to highlight how CLIP's rich visual representations reduce the burden on the mapping network and enable training with very few parameters.
suggested_caption: ClipCap maps CLIP embeddings to GPT-2 prefix tokens via a small trainable network. (Mokady et al. 2021)

description: Visualization showing that ClipCap's learned image prefix embeddings, when decoded back to text, produce words semantically related to the image content (e.g., an image of a dog yields prefix tokens interpretable as 'dog', 'animal', 'outdoor').
teaching_value: Provides intuition that the mapping network learns a meaningful semantic translation from visual to linguistic space, helping students understand that prefix embeddings are not arbitrary vectors but carry interpretable semantic content.
when_to_show: After explaining ClipCap's architecture; use as an intuition-building example to show that the learned prefix embeddings encode semantically meaningful visual concepts in language space.
suggested_caption: ClipCap prefix embeddings decode to semantically relevant words, showing visual-linguistic alignment. (Mokady et al. 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of VisualGPT showing a visual encoder feeding into a GPT-style decoder via a cross-attention mechanism with a Self-Resurrecting Activation Unit (SRAU) that uses complementary gates B_vis and B_lan to balance visual and linguistic information at each decoder layer.
teaching_value: Illustrates how gated cross-attention can dynamically balance pre-trained linguistic knowledge and new visual information at each layer, teaching students about controlled multimodal fusion beyond simple concatenation.
when_to_show: When introducing cross-attention fusion mechanisms as an alternative to prefix-based approaches; use to explain how gates B_vis and B_lan allow the model to selectively weight visual vs. linguistic signals.
suggested_caption: VisualGPT uses gated cross-attention (SRAU) to balance visual and linguistic signals per layer. (Chen et al. 2021)

description: Bar chart comparing VisualGPT against baseline models on MS COCO and Conceptual Captions datasets at 0.1% and 1% training data fractions, showing captioning performance metrics
teaching_value: Demonstrates the data efficiency advantage of adapting pre-trained LMs for vision-language tasks — students can see how VisualGPT outperforms models trained from scratch with very limited data
when_to_show: Show when discussing why pre-trained LM adaptation is preferable to training from scratch, especially in low-data regimes
suggested_caption: VisualGPT outperforms baselines with only 0.1%–1% training data. (Chen et al. 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of VC-GPT showing CLIP-ViT visual encoder feeding into cross-attention layers, a self-ensemble module combining language decoder logits and vision-language fused logits, with GPT2 as the language decoder
teaching_value: Illustrates how a pretrained vision encoder (CLIP-ViT) and pretrained language model (GPT2) are connected via cross-attention layers and a self-ensemble module to prevent catastrophic forgetting while enabling multimodal fusion
when_to_show: Show when explaining how VC-GPT avoids catastrophic forgetting by using separate cross-attention layers rather than injecting visual features directly into GPT2
suggested_caption: VC-GPT fuses CLIP-ViT and GPT2 via cross-attention with self-ensemble. (Luo et al. 2022)

description: Performance table comparing VC-GPT variants (with/without self-ensemble) against end-to-end image captioning baselines on MS COCO test set using CIDEr, BLEU, METEOR, and SPICE metrics
teaching_value: Quantifies the contribution of the self-ensemble module and shows how combining pretrained vision and language encoders achieves competitive captioning performance; helps students understand ablation study design
when_to_show: Show when discussing the importance of the self-ensemble module in VC-GPT and how to interpret standard image captioning evaluation metrics
suggested_caption: VC-GPT self-ensemble module significantly boosts captioning metrics on MS COCO. (Luo et al. 2022)

description: Two-panel diagram of MERLOT training: left panel shows contrastive frame-caption matching; right panel shows a joint vision-language transformer trained with MLM loss and temporal frame reordering task on YouTube video segments
teaching_value: Illustrates how spatial and temporal objectives are combined in video-language pretraining — students learn how contrastive and generative losses complement each other and why temporal ordering is a useful self-supervised signal
when_to_show: Show when introducing video-based vision-language models and explaining how temporal structure in videos provides additional training signal beyond static images
suggested_caption: MERLOT uses contrastive and temporal reordering objectives for video-language pretraining. (Zellers et al. 2021)

description: High-level architecture overview of Flamingo showing a pretrained vision encoder (CLIP), a Perceiver Resampler producing fixed visual tokens, and gated cross-attention layers interleaved with frozen LM layers, accepting interleaved image-text input
teaching_value: Shows the full Flamingo pipeline — students can see how the Perceiver Resampler compresses variable-length visual features and how cross-attention layers are inserted into a frozen LM to enable multimodal in-context learning without catastrophic forgetting
when_to_show: Show when introducing Flamingo as a case study in efficiently connecting pretrained vision and language models, especially before discussing the gated cross-attention mechanism in detail
suggested_caption: Flamingo connects CLIP and a frozen LM via Perceiver Resampler and gated cross-attention. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Detailed diagram and pseudo-code of Flamingo's gated cross-attention-dense layer, showing how visual tokens attend to language hidden states with a learned tanh gate that initializes to zero to preserve pretrained LM behavior
teaching_value: Explains the gated cross-attention mechanism at implementation level — the tanh gate initialized to zero is a key design choice that lets the model start as a pure LM and gradually incorporate visual information, preventing catastrophic forgetting
when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific mechanism Flamingo uses to safely inject visual information into a frozen LM, particularly the role of the learned gate initialized to zero
suggested_caption: Flamingo's tanh-gated cross-attention layer preserves pretrained LM behavior at initialization. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Line or bar charts showing Flamingo performance scaling with model size (e.g., 3B, 9B, 80B parameters) and number of few-shot examples (0-shot, 4-shot, 8-shot, 32-shot) across vision-language benchmarks
teaching_value: Demonstrates two key scaling laws for few-shot vision-language models: larger models and more in-context examples both improve performance, helping students understand the relationship between model capacity and in-context learning
when_to_show: Show when discussing how Flamingo leverages in-context learning at test time and why model scale matters for few-shot multimodal performance
suggested_caption: Flamingo performance improves with both model scale and number of few-shot examples. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Comprehensive results table comparing Flamingo (0-shot, 4-shot, 32-shot, fine-tuned) of different sizes against state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines across 16 vision-language tasks including VQA, captioning, and visual reasoning
teaching_value: Provides concrete evidence that few-shot prompting with a large enough model can match or exceed task-specific fine-tuned models, illustrating the practical value of general-purpose vision-language models
when_to_show: Show when comparing few-shot prompting versus fine-tuning paradigms, and when discussing the breadth of tasks a single vision-language model can handle
suggested_caption: Flamingo matches fine-tuned SoTA on 6/16 tasks using only few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Architecture diagram of CoCa (Contrastive Captioner) showing a shared image encoder, a unimodal text decoder for contrastive loss, and a multimodal text decoder with cross-attention for captioning loss, trained jointly from scratch
teaching_value: Illustrates how CoCa unifies contrastive and generative pretraining objectives in a single architecture — students can see how splitting the text decoder into unimodal and multimodal parts enables both CLIP-style and captioning-style learning simultaneously
when_to_show: Show when introducing CoCa and explaining how combining contrastive and captioning losses in one model captures both discriminative and generative vision-language representations
suggested_caption: CoCa jointly trains contrastive and captioning objectives with split text decoders. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)

description: Diagram showing how CoCa's pretrained model is adapted to downstream tasks (visual recognition, VQA, image captioning) using task-specific attention poolers with different numbers of learnable queries
teaching_value: Shows how a single pretrained model can be adapted to diverse downstream tasks by varying the attention pooler — students learn how pooling granularity (single embedding vs. fine-grained) affects suitability for recognition vs. understanding tasks
when_to_show: Show when discussing how CoCa handles diverse downstream tasks and the role of attention poolers as lightweight task adapters
suggested_caption: CoCa uses task-specific attention poolers to adapt to recognition and understanding tasks. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)

description: Pseudo-code block showing the CoCa (Contrastive Captioners) architecture and training procedure, including the dual-loss setup with contrastive and captioning objectives, weighted combination, and the encoder-decoder structure.
teaching_value: Allows students to see exactly how CoCa combines contrastive and captioning losses in code form, making the abstract dual-objective training concrete and implementable.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how CoCa differs from contrastive-only or captioning-only models, especially to illustrate how the weighted loss combination (λ_cap=2.0, λ_con=1.0) is implemented in practice.
suggested_caption: CoCa pseudo-code: dual contrastive and captioning loss training. (Yu & Wang et al., 2022)

description: A results table comparing MAGiC image captioning performance against other methods on COCO and Flickr30k benchmarks, broken down by supervised vs. unsupervised approaches and standard metrics (BLEU, CIDEr, SPICE, etc.).
teaching_value: Helps students understand the performance gap between training-free (unsupervised) methods like MAGiC and fully supervised approaches, grounding the theoretical trade-offs in concrete benchmark numbers.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the MAGiC decoding mechanism to contextualize its practical limitations — specifically when discussing the trade-off between no-training convenience and performance ceiling.
suggested_caption: MAGiC captioning results on COCO and Flickr30k vs. supervised baselines. (Su et al., 2022)

description: Diagram illustrating how PICa performs n-shot Visual Question Answering at inference time: an image is converted to a caption/tags, then combined with n few-shot example (image-caption, question, answer) triplets as a prompt fed into GPT-3.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the full inference pipeline of PICa — how visual information is converted to language and how in-context learning with GPT-3 is structured — making the prompt engineering strategy tangible.
when_to_show: Show when introducing PICa to explain how vision tasks can be solved by converting images to text and leveraging large language models without fine-tuning.
suggested_caption: PICa n-shot VQA: image captions + few-shot examples prompt GPT-3. (Yang et al., 2021)

description: Bar chart comparing PICa-Base (random in-context examples) vs. PICa-Full (similar example selection + multi-query ensembling) against other methods on the OK-VQA knowledge-based VQA benchmark.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the quantitative impact of two specific improvements — similar example selection and multi-query ensembling — helping students understand how prompt engineering choices directly affect performance.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the two PICa improvements to give students concrete evidence of how each design choice contributes to the +8.6 point SoTA improvement on OK-VQA.
suggested_caption: PICa-Full vs. PICa-Base on OK-VQA: impact of example selection and ensembling. (Yang et al., 2021)

description: Diagram illustrating the Socratic Models framework showing how multiple pretrained models (VLM, LM, ALM) exchange information through language as an intermediate representation, with arrows depicting the multimodal prompting chain.
teaching_value: Provides a high-level conceptual map of how Socratic Models compose heterogeneous pretrained models without additional training, making the 'language as interface' paradigm visually explicit.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the Socratic Models explanation to give students the architectural overview before diving into specific task examples.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models: language as interface composing VLM, LM, and ALM. (Zeng et al., 2022)

description: Step-by-step illustration of the Socratic Models pipeline for image captioning: VLM predicts place/object/image-type categories, these fill a structured language prompt, and a causal LM generates the final caption.
teaching_value: Makes the multi-model multimodal prompting process concrete by showing exactly how VLM outputs are inserted into a language template and passed to an LM, clarifying the 'Socratic' chaining mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific image captioning application of Socratic Models to illustrate how VLM-to-LM chaining works step by step.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models captioning: VLM fills prompt slots, LM generates caption. (Zeng et al., 2022)

description: Table comparing image captioning performance (CIDEr, SPICE, CLIP-S metrics) of Socratic Models against ClipCap and other baselines on 100 random COCO test examples, with training-free vs. trained model distinctions.
teaching_value: Quantifies the performance gap between the training-free Socratic approach and trained models like ClipCap, helping students calibrate expectations for zero-shot compositional methods.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Socratic Models captioning pipeline to ground the discussion with benchmark results and highlight the cost-performance trade-off of training-free approaches.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models vs. ClipCap on COCO captioning: training-free performance gap. (Zeng et al., 2022)

description: Diagram showing the Socratic Models pipeline for egocentric video understanding: audio is processed by ALM (speech-to-text), video frames by VLM (image-to-text), outputs are combined into a structured prompt, and an LM generates activity summaries and answers questions.
teaching_value: Extends the Socratic Models concept to a multimodal, multi-step real-world task (video + audio), showing students how the framework scales beyond static image captioning to complex temporal and cross-modal reasoning.
when_to_show: Show when demonstrating the flexibility of Socratic Models beyond image captioning, particularly to illustrate how audio, video, and language models can be chained for complex activity understanding tasks.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models for egocentric video: chaining ALM, VLM, and LM via language. (Zeng et al., 2022)
